summary: The post argues that while cultural critics have focused on technology addiction as the defining weakness of modern society, a subtler problem is emerging: the internet's capacity to make empathy selfish and performative. Using examples like Kony 2012 and a viral poverty essay where supporters didn't care about factual accuracy, Asparouhova suggests that online empathy may be 'masturbatory'—a reflexive act of emotional self-gratification rather than genuine concern for others. She poses a thought experiment about whether people would still use Reddit if all content were computer-generated, suggesting the answer reveals how disconnected online engagement is from reality. The post concludes that the internet has amplified an age-old philosophical question about whether altruism is truly selfless, and predicts this will be the next great subject for cultural satirists.
key_points: Cultural critiques of technology addiction (screen time, digital detox) are already becoming dated; deeper structural problems are emerging
The internet enables performative empathy where people engage emotionally without caring whether the content is real, as shown by Kony 2012 and viral fundraising fraud
Online empathy may be fundamentally selfish—a controlled, curated emotional experience that substitutes for engagement with the physical world
The internet lets people curate their emotional lives, choosing exactly what to feel outraged or joyful about, reducing empathy to consumption
The philosophical question of whether altruism is selfish has been amplified by the internet to a societal scale
money_quotes: As we become more empathetic to what's on our screens, we risk becoming less empathetic to the physical world around us.

It begs the question whether Internet empathy is simply masturbatory: a reflexive search to relieve our desires for joy and anger and emotion through external stimuli that could just as easily be fake as it is real.
But maybe we don't care, because we want to believe that it all matters.
The empathy that the internet has created may be purely selfish, after all.
Fiction or non-fiction, it felt good then and it feels good now.
We can limit our outrage to videos from Upworthy that match our political biases. We can follow @EmergencyPuppy on Twitter to give ourselves a daily dose of joy.
The internet has amplified that, made it harder to know when we're caring about others or just caring about ourselves.
key_insight: The internet's greatest cultural danger isn't technology addiction but the way it transforms empathy into a curated, self-serving emotional experience detached from reality.
